interest: This is driven by teachers and their unions (pedagogus_erdekvedo, pedagogus_szakszervezeti_hang) seeking that structural reform not be imposed on them without funded capacity, staffing, and autonomy protections.
value: It pits equity (abolishing early sorting) against professional dignity and feasibility (teachers' workload, autonomy, and the credibility of the reform's own equity promise if support systems are absent).
fear: The real need is protection from becoming the shock-absorber of an underfunded reform — teachers fear being held accountable for sharply increased classroom heterogeneity without paid retraining, class-size caps, or added support staff, turning comprehensiveness into a warehousing model.
affected: pupils in mixed-ability classrooms (especially disadvantaged pupils)
public comprehensive schools serving high-disadvantage populations
assumption: It assumes that without legally binding, pre-rollout costing, class-size caps, and staffing guarantees, the retraining and support will not materialize at the scale and quality S3's equity claim depends on.
empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: the scenario itself tags retraining cost as 'high' with 'no precise per-cohort unit cost,' the Finnish-reform expert flags Hungary as lacking equivalent teacher-quality/support systems, and no Hungarian precedent exists for retraining at this scale — so whether adequate capacity can be built in the timeline is unresolved.
